sources.title: Evidence synthesis: writing university-grade learning content
sources.locator: Executive conclusion and limitations; sections 4-9 and 12; evidence-to-rule matrix. This rapid, decision-oriented synthesis is not a registered systematic review, and its included studies vary in learners, materials, comparison conditions, delays, feedback, and outcomes.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Only bounded evidence conclusions and limitations are used as factual support; the lesson's organization, prose, examples, tables, prompts, and answer logic were independently created. Repository CONTENT-LICENSE.md identifies research prose as CC BY 4.0.

agentAccess.notes: Repository-owned source explicitly supplied for this author run. The repository content license includes research prose, and no access control or term prohibiting agent processing was found. This is a dated observation, not a source-owner warranty.

sources.title: EmbeddedKnowledge Learning-Content Standard v1
sources.locator: Project-internal authoring contract, sections 2-8 and 12-15. This is an EmbeddedKnowledge design convention authored by the same project and accountable principal, not an external standards-body publication or independent empirical source.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only to identify project-internal design conventions and their stated limits; it is not treated as independent empirical evidence. No source wording, examples, question sequence, or expressive structure is copied. Repository CONTENT-LICENSE.md identifies CONTENT-STANDARD.md as CC BY 4.0.

agentAccess.notes: Repository-owned project contract explicitly required for this author run. The repository content license includes this file, and no access control or term prohibiting agent processing was found. Its internal origin is disclosed so it is not mistaken for external authority.

sources.title: Test-Enhanced Learning: Taking Memory Tests Improves Long-Term Retention
sources.locator: Psychological Science 17(3), 249-255; DOI 10.1111/j.1467-9280.2006.01693.x. Primary experiments with prose passages compared repeated study and retrieval at immediate and delayed tests; the narrow tasks and delays do not establish a universal effect for every learner or assessment.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license confirmed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the general factual contrast between retrieval and repeated study across the reported delayed tests. No article prose, example, table, figure, or item is quoted, adapted, redistributed, or mirrored.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified the DOI and citation metadata through Crossref's public REST record. Neither route required login or bypassing access controls; publisher full text was not opened.

sources.title: Rethinking the Use of Tests: A Meta-Analysis of Practice Testing
sources.locator: Review of Educational Research 87(3), 659-701; DOI 10.3102/0034654316689306. Meta-analysis of practice testing across varied formats and conditions; heterogeneity in tests, feedback, learner populations, and final measures limits any single task prescription.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license confirmed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only as synthesis-level factual support that retrieval practice has a positive average effect across heterogeneous educational comparisons. No source expression, examples, instruments, tables, figures, or datasets are reused.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the abstract and bibliographic metadata deposited in Crossref's public REST API and did not open publisher full text. Crossref states that its bibliographic metadata is publicly available and almost none is subject to copyright; no source expression was reused.

sources.title: Distributed Practice in Verbal Recall Tasks: A Review and Quantitative Synthesis
sources.locator: Psychological Bulletin 132(3), 354-380; DOI 10.1037/0033-2909.132.3.354. Quantitative synthesis of distributed verbal-recall practice, including the relation between spacing gap and desired retention interval; it does not specify one gap for all materials or retrieval plans.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license displayed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for factual support that distributed practice generally benefits later retention and that useful gaps depend partly on the retention interval. No wording, study materials, figures, tables, or data are reused.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified DOI metadata through Crossref. The publisher article was not opened; no login or access control was bypassed. The abstract itself distinguishes distributed learning episodes from a universal retrieval schedule.

sources.title: Similarity Matters: A Meta-Analysis of Interleaved Learning and Its Moderators
sources.locator: Psychological Bulletin 145(11), 1029-1052; DOI 10.1037/bul0000209. Meta-analysis of interleaved learning and moderators, including material similarity and task type; average effects do not make interleaving a universal replacement for supported acquisition.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license displayed in the Crossref record

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the bounded factual claim that interleaving effects vary and are relevant to discrimination among confusable categories or strategies. No article expression, tasks, tables, figures, or data are reused.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified DOI metadata through Crossref. The abstract reports the material and task moderators recorded here. Publisher full text was not opened, and no access control was bypassed.

sources.title: The Power of Feedback Revisited: A Meta-Analysis of Educational Feedback Research
sources.locator: Frontiers in Psychology volume 10 (volume year 2019), article 3087; electronically published 2020-01-22; DOI 10.3389/fpsyg.2019.03087. Meta-analysis of educational feedback with substantial heterogeneity; it distinguishes information about task, process, and self-regulation from person-directed comments but does not prescribe one universal script or timing.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for general factual support about information-rich educational feedback and its heterogeneous effects. Although the article is CC BY 4.0, no article wording, table, figure, or intervention is copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the PubMed abstract through NCBI E-utilities within the published request limits and verified the DOI, date, publisher, and CC BY 4.0 record through Crossref. The abstract reports the heterogeneity and information-content moderation recorded here; no protected expression was reused.

sources.title: A Meta-Analysis of the Worked Examples Effect on Mathematics Performance
sources.publisher: Springer Science and Business Media LLC
sources.locator: Educational Psychology Review 35(1), article 11; DOI 10.1007/s10648-023-09745-1; ERIC EJ1364058. Meta-analysis of 55 studies and 181 effects on mathematics performance from elementary through postsecondary settings; it supports a worked-example default within that scope, not this lesson's exact planning sequence.
sources.license: Publisher copyright; no open redistribution license confirmed in the Crossref or ERIC metadata

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the factual claim that worked examples have a positive average effect in the reviewed mathematics settings. The lesson's fictional case, procedure, prompts, tables, and five-stage practice progression are independently authored and do not reproduce study materials.

agentAccess.notes: The public ERIC record and Crossref metadata were inspected for title, authors, date, DOI, study count, population range, and abstract-level limitations; publisher full text was not opened. No term prohibiting this limited public metadata access was found.

sources.title: A Cornerstone of Adaptivity: A Meta-Analysis of the Expertise Reversal Effect
sources.locator: Learning and Instruction 98, article 102142; DOI 10.1016/j.learninstruc.2025.102142. Meta-analysis of assistance-by-prior-knowledge interactions across 60 studies; effects vary by learner and domain and support adaptive assistance, not a fixed sequence for every learner.

uses.rightsBasisEvidence: Used only for the general factual principle that the useful amount of instructional assistance depends on demonstrated prior knowledge. No source wording, framework, table, figure, or study material is copied or adapted.

agentAccess.notes: The authoring agent inspected the publicly accessible peDOCS citation and abstract record and verified the DOI, authors, date, journal, article number, and CC BY 4.0 license through Crossref. No login, access control, robots prohibition, or displayed term restricting this limited record access was found; publisher full text was not copied.
